title: Refining classification of complex agroforestry mosaic landscape from drone-based imagery: implications for landscape management and conservation
abstract:
1. Accurately and efficiently mapping landscape compositions is a conservation priority as natural habitats that are reservoirs of biodiversity are increasingly lost or modified. Globally, agroforestry is a rising sustainable land use type that benefits landowners and biodiversity, but these land-use land-cover (LULC) mosaics can be challenging to map due to the size and distribution of different patches of LULC, requiring improved methodologies to map these areas and refine landscape-based conservation management strategies.

2. We classified an agroforestry landscape in West Java, Indonesia, which includes the habitat of a Critically Endangered primate, the Javan slow loris (Nycticebus javanicus). We used object-based image analysis of high-resolution drone (DJI Phantom 4, 2.87 cm/px mean resolution) and satellite (PlanetLabs, 3 m resolution) imagery, comparing how the resulting classifications differ and impact classification of loris GPS waypoints.

3. Estimations of LULC types significantly differed between the classified drone and satellite imagery, affecting all habitat types. Compared to classifications from drone imagery, satellite imagery-based classifications overestimated forest-cover by an average of 18.81% and chayote (a local vining crop) by 16.02%, while underestimating non-chayote agriculture by 18.72%. Specific crop types were also classified from drone imagery that were impossible to classify using satellite imagery.

4. Image classification differences are reflected in significantly different classifications of loris GPS waypoints, with an overestimation of the amount of waypoints in forest and chayote, and underestimation of waypoints in non-chayote agriculture.

5. Synthesis and applications: Together, these results show that significant differences in landscape classification between high-resolution satellite and drone imagery can substantially change our understanding of slow loris habitat availability. Thus, it can be beneficial to use the highest resolution imagery to characterize species’ habitats, whenever possible, particularly within mosaic landscapes. Additionally, image and map resolution should be explicitly reported. Less accurate area estimations of LULC types, particularly for forest and agriculture, directly impact conservation planning because species persistence may rely on these habitat patches and restoration of connectivity between them. Changes to forest and agriculture LULC can reflect the socioeconomic processes driving landscape changes.

R E S E A R C H  A R T I C L E
Refining classification of complex agroforestry mosaic 
landscape from drone-­based imagery: Implications for 
landscape management and conservation
Leslie Annette Paige1
 |   Katherine Hedger2,3 |   Esther Adinda2 |   Endah Sulistyawati4 |   
Alexander S. Bradley5 |   Bill Winston5 |   Karen E. DeMatteo6
 |   K. A. I. Nekaris2,3
 |   
Emily E. Wroblewski1

Sciences, Centre for Ecology, Evolution and Environment, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge, UK; 4Institut Teknologi Bandung, Bandung, Java, Indonesia; 
5Department of Earth, Environmental and Planetary Sciences, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, Missouri, USA and 6Department of Biology,

K. A. I. Nekaris

Handling Editor: Javier Lopatin
Abstract
1. Accurately and efficiently mapping landscape compositions is a conservation pri-
ority as natural habitats that are reservoirs of biodiversity are increasingly lost or 
modified. Globally, agroforestry is a rising sustainable land-­use type that benefits 
landowners and biodiversity, but these land-­use land-­cover (LULC) mosaics can 
be challenging to map due to the size and distribution of different LULC patches, 
requiring improved methodologies to map these areas and refine landscape-­
based conservation management strategies.
2. We classified an agroforestry landscape in West Java, Indonesia, which includes 
the habitat of a Critically Endangered primate, the Javan slow loris (Nycticebus 
javanicus). We used object-­based image analysis of high-­resolution drone (DJI 
Phantom 4 Pro, 2.90 cm/px mean resolution) and satellite (PlanetLabs, 3 m resolu-
tion) imagery, comparing how the resulting classifications differ and impact the 
classification of GPS waypoints collected during focal animal follows.
3. Estimations of LULC types significantly differed between the classified drone 
and satellite imagery, affecting all habitat types. Compared to classifications from 
drone imagery, satellite imagery-­based classifications overestimated forest cover 
by an average of 18.81% and chayote (a vining crop) by 16.02%, while underesti-
mating non-­chayote agriculture by 18.72%. Specific crop types were also classi-
fied from drone imagery that were impossible to classify using satellite imagery.
4.	 Image classification differences are reflected in significantly different classifi-
cations of loris GPS waypoints, with an overestimation of the amount of way-
points in forest and chayote, and underestimation of waypoints in non-­chayote 
agriculture.
2 of 14 |    
PAIGE et al.
1  |  INTRODUCTION
Globally, human-­induced land cover change is occurring at rapid 
rates, with immense consequences for a myriad of ecological, en-
vironmental and economic processes (Foley et  al., 2005), particu-
larly with loss of biodiversity contributing to local and global climate 
change and ecosystem disruption (Winkler et al., 2021). Land cover 
is changing largely via large-­scale deforestation, urbanization and 
agriculture expansion that reduces natural habitats (Lambin & 
Meyfroidt, 2011; Radwan et al., 2021). The outcomes are landscapes 
that are fragmented and composed of complex mixtures of land-­use 
types (referred to as heterogeneous or mosaic landscapes), which 
pose a large threat to the persistence of global biodiversity (Durán 
et al., 2020; Newbold et al., 2015). With growing human populations 
and demand for resources, rates of land conversion and associated 
biodiversity loss are projected to increase in coming years (Jacobson 
et al., 2019; Semenchuk et al., 2022).
A growing global practice to mitigate the impacts of habitat loss is 
the adoption of sustainable landscape management plans that bene-
fit both human and wildlife populations. Agroforestry is a sustainable 
mosaic landscape, where trees and shrubs are grown alongside crops 
or pastures, providing economic benefits for landowners and useable 
habitats for native wildlife (Nair et al., 2021). Monitoring and mapping 
patterns of agroforestry and other land uses is a high priority for stake-
holders since designing effective management strategies requires an 
accurate understanding of the landscape (Foley et al., 2005; Turner 
2nd et al., 2007). Satellite imagery is often used to classify landscapes, 
and the variation in resolution of available satellite imagery may mean 
there is sufficient resolution to assess landscape heterogeneity and 
species' needs in some circumstances. However, many mosaic land-
scapes are challenging to accurately map as they vary on spatial 
scales smaller than can be captured with satellite imagery, and their 
different components have high spectral similarity (Gibril et al., 2020; 
Hurskainen et al., 2019; Mercier et al., 2019). Consequently, smaller 
but ecologically significant patches or corridors of vegetation ex-
isting outside of larger forest tracts, like in agroforestry landscapes, 
are often excluded from vegetation estimates (Lechner et al., 2009; 
Räsänen & Virtanen, 2019; Reiner et al., 2023). It is necessary to quan-
tify these features to track and improve the predictions of global land-­
use trends and facilitate wildlife persistence in modified landscapes 
(Lindenmayer, 2019; Wintle et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2021).
Imagery from unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs or drones) has 
great potential to be incorporated into landscape monitoring and 
wildlife habitat mapping as UAVs can capture imagery at a higher 
resolution than satellites, down to cm/px (Koh & Wich,  2012). 
However, UAV imagery has been minimally used to map animal 
habitats (Han et al., 2017; Stark et al., 2018; Szantoi et al., 2017). 
Improving the accuracy and specificity of land cover classification 
can change our estimation of the total area and distribution of land 
cover types, which, in turn, can change our understanding of how 
endangered species use habitats, as well as how best to protect the 
species and the habitats they depend on. Therefore, this study in-
vestigates whether drone-­based imagery improves classifications of 
a highly human-­modified, complex, agroforestry mosaic landscape 
in Java (Indonesia).
1. We compared land-­use land-­cover (LULC) classifications from 
high-­resolution drone (mean resolution of 2.87 cm/px) and high-­
resolution satellite imagery (3 m). Java is an island experiencing 
rapid rates of land conversion while having one of the highest 
human population densities in the world (Jones, 2013; Pravitasari 
et al., 2024). Agroforestry is the dominant land-­use type across 
the island, followed by monoculture crops, making refining the 
ability to classify these landscapes important for the develop-
ment of conservation strategies for Java's vulnerable wildlife 
populations (Purwaningsih et  al.,  2020; Udawatta et  al., 2021).
5.	 Synthesis and applications. Together, these results show that significant differ-
ences in landscape classification between high-­resolution satellite and drone 
imagery can substantially change our understanding of slow loris habitat avail-
ability and use. Thus, it can be beneficial to use the highest resolution imagery 
to characterize species' habitats, whenever possible, particularly within mosaic 
landscapes. Additionally, image and map resolution should be explicitly reported. 
Less accurate area estimations of LULC types, particularly for forest and agricul-
ture, directly impact conservation planning because species persistence may rely 
on these habitat patches and restoration of connectivity between them. Changes 
to forest and agriculture LULC can reflect the socio-­economic processes driving 
landscape changes.
K E Y W O R D S
drone imagery, satellite imagery, remote-­sensing, object-­based image analysis, Javan slow loris, 
habitat mapping and availability, habitat use

PAIGE et al.
2. We then tested whether differences in classification from the two 
image sources change classifications of GPS waypoints collected 
during focal animal follows of the Critically Endangered Javan 
slow loris (Nycticebus javanicus) because GPS waypoints, and as-
sociated LULC characteristics, are often incorporated in analyses 
of animal–habitat relationships. The Javan slow loris is a strictly 
arboreal primate that is sensitive to fine scale landscape variation 
because individuals have relatively small home ranges and rely 
on vegetation connectivity to navigate the landscape (Nekaris 
et al., 2017). Loss of forest habitat has contributed to the Javan 
slow loris becoming one of the 25 most endangered primates in 
the world (Mittermeier et al., 2022). However, like many species 
on Java, lorises persist by using human-­modified landscapes that 
are adjacent to forest habitats, which makes classifying these 
complex, dynamic landscapes, as well as understanding how 
species use or avoid them, priorities to promote and maximize 
human–biodiversity coexistence (Nekaris et al., 2017).
2  |  METHODS
2.1  |  Study site and loris population
Research was conducted at the Little Fireface Project (LFP) field 
research site in Cipaganti, Cisurupan, Garut Regency, West Java, 
Indonesia (Figure 1a,b). Drone surveys were deemed exempt by 
Washington University in St. Louis' Institutional Animal Care 
and Use Committee (IACUC) and conducted under research 
permit number 365/SIP/IV/FR/6/2023 from Indonesia's National 
Research and Innovation Agency. Javan slow loris behavioural 
research was conducted according to the guidelines of the 
Declaration of Helsinki and followed the American Society of 
Primatologists Principles for the Ethical Treatment of Non-­human 
Primates. It was approved by the Animal Ethics Subcommittee 
of Oxford Brookes University permit number 12OXASA2724. 
The landscape is a mosaic of human development, agriculture, 
and trees that exist either in rows to demarcate fields or in scat-
tered patches (Nekaris et al., 2017). The main source of income 
for Cipaganti residents is agriculture, with available land used 
for that purpose. Dominant crops include coffee (Coffea arabica), 
tea (Camellia sinesis), tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) and chayote 
(Sechium edule) (Nekaris et al., 2017). Chayote, a vining crop grown 
on elevated bamboo lattice frames, provides substrate that the 
strictly arboreal Javan slow loris can traverse (Nekaris et al., 2017) 
(Figure 2).
Javan slow lorises live in single male-­single female family 
groups, which may include 1–4 offspring (Nekaris et  al.,  2020). 
This nocturnal species is highly territorial, with venom used for 
defence, including during intraspecific competition; consequently, 
neighbouring family groups have very little home range overlap 
(Nekaris et  al.,  2020) (Figure  1c). One year (August 2022–July 
2023) of ranging data, collected by LFP, included nine loris fam-
ily groups, totalling 16 lorises. Focal individuals were followed 
nightly (1700–0500 h), six nights a week and GPS waypoints were 
collected using a handheld Garmin unit at 5-­min intervals (Birot 
et al., 2020).
FI G U R E 1 (a) Location of the island of Java, Indonesia. (b) Location of Cipaganti in West Java. (c) One-­year, 100% minimum convex 
polygon home ranges of nine loris family groups at the study site in Cipaganti overlaid on 3 m satellite imagery from 29 July 2023. Panels (a) 
and (b) © RNaturalEarth; Image (c) © 2023 Planet Labs PBC.

4 of 14 |    
PAIGE et al.
2.2  |  Drone and satellite imagery acquisition
2.2.1  |  Drone surveys
GPS data collected during focal animal follows over the preceding 
year defined the 100% minimum convex polygon home ranges sur-
veyed with a DJI Phantom 4 Pro drone in July 2023 (Figure 1c). We 
conducted eight surveys, totalling 117 ha, from 18 to 29 July, over 
the nine loris family group home ranges (ranges 5 and 6 surveyed 
together). Prior to each survey, an Emlid Reach RS2 GNSS unit in 
the WGS 84/UTM Zone 48S reference system recorded six ground 
control points (GCPs) per survey area, ensuring accurately geo-
referenced drone imagery (Martínez-­Carricondo et  al.,  2018) (see 
Supporting Information for more detail).
2.2.2  |  Satellite imagery
We acquired 3 m resolution satellite imagery of Cipaganti from 
Planet Labs (Planet Team,  2017). Imagery was captured with the 
PlanetScope constellation of DOVE satellites. Although the satellites 
take daily images, we used images from 29 July 2023, the closest day 
to the surveys without cloud cover. Imagery was downloaded with 
analytic radiance (TOAR) to adjust for atmospheric radiance, result-
ing in four bands. Only RGB bands were analysed to be comparable 
with the drone imagery.
2.3  |  Data processing
GCPs were post-­processed using the Canadian Spatial Reference 
System Precise Point Positioning (NRCan-­PPP), which improves spa-
tial positioning (Natural Resources Canada,  2017). We processed 
drone imagery using AgiSoft Metashape, in which individual pho-
tographs were aligned to create a single, unified orthomosaic map 
of the survey area (Gibril et al., 2020; Kamarudin & Wei, 2022). We 
then georeferenced the map by manually entering and identifying 
GCP coordinates.
2.4  |  Data analyses
2.4.1  |  Machine learning landscape classification
We used object-­based image analysis (OBIA) in Catalyst 
Professional to classify landscape types in the drone orthomosaic 
maps and satellite imagery. Briefly, OBIA classifies imagery based 
on pixel groups, or discreet ‘objects’, grouped together based upon 
spectral, shape and texture variables (Blaschke, 2010; Hossain & 
Chen, 2019) (see Supporting Information for more detail). Satellite 
imagery was classified as forest, chayote, non-­chayote agriculture 
and developed land (e.g. housing and other structures) (Figure 3). 
Non-­chayote agriculture was further distinguished in the drone 
imagery as row crops (e.g. chilies, tomatoes, cabbages) or dot 
crops (e.g. coffee, tea).
Image classification accuracy was assessed using a confusion ma-
trix, which compares locations on the ground of known use (‘ground 
truth’) to the image classification (Manohar et al., 2021). Here, drone 
imagery classification was used as the ground truth to which sat-
ellite classification was compared using 100 randomly stratified 
accuracy assessment points in each of the nine loris home ranges 
(Li et al., 2024; Quattrini et al., 2025), following the recommended 
50–100 assessment points per LULC class (Congalton,  2001; 
Foody, 2002).
2.4.2  |  Loris home range and GPS waypoint 
classification comparison
We defined 100% minimum convex home range polygons for each of 
the nine loris family groups using the Minimum Bounding Geometry 
tool in ArcGIS and all GPS waypoints from August 2022 to September 
2023, given that this species is highly territorial with stable home 
ranges. The resulting polygons were overlaid on the classified habitat 
type maps in ArcGIS, and full maps clipped to the home range areas 
for analyses. For each home range, the area of each LULC type was 
calculated from the classified satellite and drone imagery. To com-
pare classification proportions of GPS waypoints collected during 
FI G U R E 2 Chayote (Sechium edule) is a vining crop grown on elevated bamboo lattice frames (left, © Leslie Annette Paige) that provides 
the only elevated substrate outside of forest that the arboreal Javan slow loris can traverse (middle). This contrasts their natural forest 
habitat (right) (middle, right © Little Fireface Project).
 13652664, 2026, 2, Downloaded from https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/1365-2664.70290 by Anglia Ruskin University, Wiley Online Library on [20/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
    |  5 of 14
PAIGE et al.
loris focal follows, the waypoints (total = 32,142, mean = 3571 points 
per home range [range: 1901–7202]) were overlaid on both the clas-
sified drone and satellite maps, and the LULC type for each point was 
extracted. We excluded points that fell on borders between LULC 
types.
2.4.3  |  Statistical analyses
We used Chi-­squared and Fisher's exact tests to test for differences 
in home range LULC classification and loris waypoint classifications 
based on imagery source. Drone-­based classifications of row and dot 
crop were combined and analysed as ‘non-­chayote agriculture’ to 
allow comparison with satellite imagery (Hsu et al., 2020). Analyses 
used the total, aggregated data from all loris home ranges and were 
conducted in R 4.4.2.
3  |  RESULTS
3.1  |  Orthomosaic maps
The drone collected 2970 photos over the eight surveys covering nine 
loris home ranges (mean survey area = 13 ha; range: 6–30 ha), of which 
2915 images were successfully aligned (98.14%) to generate eight or-
thomosaic maps (Figure  4). Mean map image resolution among the 
eight survey areas was 2.87 cm/px (range: 2.28–4.31 cm/px).
3.2  |  Satellite and drone imagery LULC 
classification
LULC classification significantly differed between satellite and drone 
imagery (χ2 [3, N = 18] = 2559.80, p < 0.005) and affected all LULC 
types (Fisher's exact tests, all comparisons p < 0.005) (Table 1; Figure 4; 
Figure S1; Table S1). Satellite classification overestimated forest in six of 
nine loris ranges by a mean of 18.81% (range: 4.88%–37.35%) (Figures 5 
and 6). Satellite classification estimated forest comprised a mean of 
47.07% of loris home ranges, compared to 36.06% from drone classifica-
tion. Similarly, chayote was overestimated in six of nine ranges by a mean 
of 16.02% (range: 2.98%–42.98%), with chayote accounting for a mean 
of 35.15% of loris range area from satellite classification but 25.54% from 
drone classification. Satellite classification underestimated non-­chayote 
agriculture in eight of nine ranges by a mean of 16.76% (range: 4.87%–
25.38%), with non-­chayote agriculture comprising a mean of 12.41% of 
loris ranges for satellite classification but 32.72% from drone classifica-
tion. In all ranges, the total non-­chayote agricultural area classified from 
satellite imagery was less than that identified via drone classification, 
suggesting that drone-­based classification more accurately identifies 
total agricultural land (in addition to chayote), regardless of the ability to 
differentiate between row and dot crops. Developed land was the least 
over-­ or underestimated from satellite imagery (overestimation range: 
0.14%–5.45%, underestimation range: 0.21%–3.49%), but these esti-
mated differences are notable given developed land consistently com-
prised a low proportion of the home range (0%–5.47%), except in Group 
8 (satellite: 29.66%; drone: 31.68%).
FI G U R E 3 Representative images of discernible land-­use land-­cover (LULC) classifications from 3 m satellite imagery (top, © Planet Labs 
PBC) and DJI Phantom 4 Pro drone surveys (bottom). Non-­chayote agricultural land identified via satellite imagery was classified into row 
and dot crops using drone imagery.

PAIGE et al.
Confusion matrices indicated a low kappa coefficient (over-
all accuracy) of the satellite compared to drone classifications 
(mean = 27.77% ± SD 11.07% agreement between the two classi-
fications) (Figure 7; Tables S2–S10). Forest was classified most ac-
curately (mean = 66.11% ± SD 22.12%), followed closely by chayote 
(mean = 58.55% ± SD 21.25%), but both still had a low overall accuracy 
(<70%). Satellite imagery classified non-­chayote agricultural and de-
veloped land with <30% accuracy (agriculture: mean = 26.33% ± SD 
15.00%; developed: mean = 26.12% ± SD 29.52%). Agriculture was 
most often misclassified as developed area, forest as non-­chayote 
agriculture, developed land as chayote and chayote as non-­chayote 
agriculture.
3.3  |  Classification of loris GPS waypoints
The proportion of GPS waypoints collected during loris focal fol-
lows that associated with different LULC types differed between 
FI G U R E 4 Orthomosaic maps generated from the drone surveys of the loris home ranges. Group numbers correspond to those in 
Figure 1, with Groups 5 and 6 surveyed together. For each group and survey, the image resolution (cm/px) and total area (ha) are noted.

PAIGE et al.
the classification derived from satellite and drone imagery (χ2 [3, 
N = 18] = 3135.70, p < 0.005) and affected all LULC types (Fisher's 
exact tests, all comparisons p < 0.005) (Table 2; Figure 5; Figure S2). 
Consistent with the LULC classification differences between 
satellite and drone imagery, loris habitat use of forest and chay-
ote was overestimated while use of non-­chayote agriculture was 
underestimated.
4  |  DISCUSSION
LULC area estimations differed between satellite versus drone im-
agery classifications in the complex agroforestry mosaic landscape 
in Cipaganti, Java, Indonesia. Although we expected improved 
classification from drone imagery because of its higher resolution 
compared to the satellite imagery, such differences can change our

understanding of land use by both wildlife and people as well as re-
sulting management and conservation decisions. Accordingly, these 
differences in LULC classification resulted in different LULC propor-
tions of the Javan slow loris GPS waypoints, which could impact our 
understanding of their habitat use and behaviour.
4.1  |  Mosaic landscapes: Importance of 
resolution and causes of misclassification
Globally, forests are one of the most important natural resources, 
making accurate mapping of forest cover a priority for mitigating 
climate change and supporting REDD+ (Bonan,  2008; Canadell & 
Raupach, 2008). Assessments via satellite imagery often fail to fully 
capture trees existing outside of large forest tracts, which could be 
due to tree crowns being smaller than what is detectable via the 
image resolution and/or spectral similarity with other LULC types 
(Brandt et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2023; Schnell et al., 2015; Thomas 
et al., 2021). These issues likely contributed to the common over-
estimation of forest availability within our study area when using 
high-­resolution satellite imagery.
Emerging advanced remote sensing tools and classification 
methods have been shown to improve forest availability estimates 
(Illarionova et  al.,  2024; Lechner et  al.,  2020; Mugabowindekwe 
et al., 2023; Reiner et al., 2023). For example, forest estimates can 
be improved when vegetation indices, such as NDVI, calculated from 
multi-­ and hyperspectral imagery bands (e.g. UV, near infrared) are 
combined with RGB imagery (Pu & Landry, 2012). This study relied 
on high-­resolution, standalone RGB imagery for landscape classi-
fication, with a significant benefit of it being more cost-­effective 
and straightforward to process than other imagery (Onishi & 
Ise, 2021). Drones also show great potential to monitor vegetation, 
with the ability to identify individual trees and other plant species 
(Bertacchi et al., 2019; Cruzan et al., 2016; Weinstein et al., 2021). 
Consequently, forest monitoring could benefit from incorporating 
RGB drone surveys to better estimate trees existing in and out of 
forest tracts (Liu et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2016).
A 3 m satellite imagery underestimated non-­chayote agricul-
tural land, overestimated chayote and could not distinguish ad-
ditional crop types. This may be due to spectral similarity among 
crop types and the inability to detect different (earlier) growing 
stages (Wu et al., 2023). However, high-­resolution drone imagery 
and OBIA enabled finer separation of different agricultural crop 
types: chayote, dot (coffee and tea) and row crops (chillies, toma-
toes and cabbages). Although this higher resolution was critical for 
distinguishing objects for LULC classification, differences in sen-
sors and processing factors such as satellite imagery atmospheric 
corrections likely also contributed to classification differences for 
agriculture or other land uses between drone and satellite imag-
ery. Distinguishing specific crop types versus generically identify-
ing agricultural land is important because crops can have different 
meanings and values (economic and social), and their different 
structural characteristics also provide different habitats (Masolele 
et  al.,  2024). Additionally, agroforestry is widely considered a 
more sustainable form of agriculture than monoculture crops be-
cause it provides more habitat availability for a variety of species 
(Santos et  al.,  2022). While crop monitoring will continue to be 
a complex topic that has challenges of scalability, incorporating 
drone surveys can improve local agricultural monitoring (Murugan 
et al., 2017; Xiang & Tian, 2011).

PAIGE et al.
4.2  |  Implications for loris conservation
Historically, the Critically Endangered Javan slow loris lived in 
Indonesia's widespread lowland forests, but <9% of this habitat 
remains, and it continues to shrink due to extensive human-­driven 
habitat modification (Global Forest Watch, 2023). Remaining loris 
habitat is mainly mosaic, where small vegetation patches facilitate 
the persistence of this arboreal species. Distinguishing chayote 
from other agriculture types is important for slow lorises because 
the elevated bamboo frames used to grow chayote provide con-
nectivity for lorises to travel within their home ranges. Satellite 
imagery overestimated the area of forest and chayote, and that 
could cause overestimation of their use by the loris, as suggested 
by differences in waypoint assignments to each LULC type. This 
overestimation could bias any subsequent behaviour and habitat 
use analyses. It is vital to refine the Javan slow loris' use of non-­
chayote agriculture areas since they can exhibit terrestrial behav-
iour in the absence of tree and/or chayote connectivity, but this 
puts them at high risk of predation (Karimloo et al., 2023; Nekaris 
et  al.,  2024). Refined habitat maps can also prioritize areas for 
habitat connectivity and installation of other mitigations, such as 
canopy bridges (Birot et al., 2020).
Many conservation initiatives focus on mitigating the effects 
of habitat loss and fragmentation through actions that restore

PAIGE et al.
habitats, increase areas under protection and promote connectiv-
ity (Mokany et  al., 2020). In concert with animal behaviour data, 
landscape data from remote sensing and ground surveys underlie 
analyses used to determine priority areas for landscape manage-
ment plans (Schmitt et al., 2009). Therefore, over-­ or underestima-
tion of LULC, as observed in this study, has important ramifications 
for how we understand and protect endangered species' habitat. 
Small vegetation patches are important for protecting global bio-
diversity as, in many landscapes, patches may be all that remain 
of the original habitat and serve as footholds for endemic wildlife 
(Lindenmayer, 2019; Wintle et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2021). Threats 
faced by many species also occur on localized scales such as small-­
scale resource extraction, illegal hunting and deforestation (Estrada 
et al., 2017). Although there is increasing support for using innova-
tive methods of non-­invasive habitat monitoring to conserve bio-
diversity (Koh & Wich, 2012; Piel et al., 2021), drones have been 
minimally used to classify animal habitats (Han et al., 2017; Stark 
et al., 2018; Szantoi et al., 2017). The increased accuracy provided 
by drone-­based imagery has great potential to improve habitat as-
sessments, particularly given that many endangered species exist 
in small pockets within increasingly fragmented and mosaic land-
scapes (Schaffer-­Smith et al., 2016).
5  |  CONCLUSION
The persistence of animal and human populations is interrelated, and 
the path towards conserving biodiversity requires attention to the 
needs and challenges of both. Because a large driver of global defor-
estation is land conversion for commodities and agriculture (Curtis 
et al., 2018), the design of land management strategies that jointly 
provide resources for human populations and habitats to sustain 
biodiversity is needed (Weiss et al., 2020). Better quantification of 
LULC trends can improve the design of sustainability measures, in 
partnership with local populations and better support the needs of 
both people and wildlife.
Drones offer a powerful mapping option and may be particularly 
effective for monitoring mosaic landscapes where variation occurs 
on smaller spatial scales than can be captured with satellite imagery. 
Differences in satellite imagery resolution, including higher avail-
able resolutions than featured in this study, may additionally result 
in classification differences. As satellite imagery can be purchased 
or freely available, cover large areas with frequent pass rates and 
is available at a variety of resolutions, drones may not be a replace-
ment but rather a complementary tool that can be used to validate 
satellite imagery and train models to extrapolate findings to larger 
areas (Ventura et al., 2022). Drone surveys also have considerations 
such as reducing disturbance to wildlife, appropriate ground survey 
design, survey planning to minimize sources of image distortion and 
error that can be introduced via flight parameters (e.g. height), cam-
era specifications and settings (e.g. image overlap), aerial imagery 
(e.g. view-­angle distortion, parallax, relief displacement) and or-
thomosaic generation (e.g. stitching artefacts) (Seifert et al., 2019; 
Young et al., 2022). Selecting both imagery source (drone or satellite) 
and resolution will vary based upon the specific characteristics of 
the study landscape and wildlife in question. As classified landscape 
maps are taken at face value as accurate assessments and reused for 
a variety of disciplines, spatial error or classification discrepancies 
present within the maps can have large ‘downstream effects’ for any 
analyses that rely on those data (Estes et al., 2018). These discrep-
ancies can have real-­world implications for landscape management 
decisions. Therefore, it is essential to report the spatial resolution of 
imagery used, as well as any processing techniques that may intro-
duce additional error (Lechner et al., 2012).

DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
Data including classified drone imagery, loris home 100% minimum 
convex polygon range outlines and summarized data derived from 
the drone and satellite imagery are available in Zenodo https://​doi.​
org/​10.​5281/​zenodo.​18246374 (Paige et  al.,  2026). Orthomosaic 
maps generated from the drone surveys will be available upon re-
quest due to privacy concerns given the resolution of the imagery. 
Loris ranging data will also be available upon request to remain com-
pliant with Indonesian regulations. We do not have permission to 
share Planet imagery or associated primary outputs.
